tools.name: ChatGPT
tools.description: Used as a conversational agent (version 5.2 in thinking mode) for challenging, brainstorming, and refining explanatory accounts in the MINS method

tools.name: TERA (Evidence Review Accelerator)
tools.description: Bond University tool suite for accelerating conventional PRISMA-compliant systematic reviews, including tools for scoping, screening, deduplication, and data extraction

terms.definition: A 5-step hermeneutic approach to systematic review that uses AI as a conversational agent to synthesise complex, overwhelming evidence
A narrative approach to evidence synthesis that seeks clarity and understanding through iterative interpretation, rather than statistical aggregation
Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses — a standardised framework for conducting and reporting aggregative systematic reviews
Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome — the standard question format for PRISMA-compliant systematic reviews
Standard formal tool for assessing study quality in systematic reviews, classifying dimensions of bias as red/amber/green
MINS alternative to risk-of-bias tools that identifies specific causal mechanisms through which biases operate in studies, rather than generic quality checklists
A visual diagram mapping causal pathways with temporal ordering (before, during, after intervention) to structure the explanatory account
Clusters of similar studies grouped by study design (e.g., household studies, mass gatherings, RCTs) for within-group synthesis before overall synthesis
The search stopping criterion in MINS: continuing to search until no new hypotheses or causal pathways are found, rather than exhaustive study retrieval
A standard visual output of meta-analysis showing individual study effects and an overall average effect size with confidence interval
One mechanism by which masks may work: reducing the exhaled dose of pathogen and slowing/redirecting the infectious plume from the wearer
Mathematical estimation of how identified biases might quantitatively shift a study's results toward or away from the null
